items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.q: If E°cell for a redox reaction is positive, what does that tell you?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — but it may still be very slow if kinetic barriers are high.
items.misconception: A positive E°cell guarantees the reaction will happen rapidly at room temperature.
items.random_wrong: A positive E°cell means the reaction is exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students equate 'feasible' with 'fast', missing the thermodynamics-vs-kinetics distinction (same error as with negative ΔG).

items.q: How does a fuel cell differ from a conventional battery?
items.correct: A fuel cell continuously consumes externally supplied fuel (e.g. hydrogen) and oxidant; a battery has a fixed amount of reactants that get used up.
items.misconception: Fuel cells and batteries are identical — both just store electrical energy.
items.random_wrong: Fuel cells burn fuel to generate heat which is then converted to electricity.
items.why: Students think 'fuel' implies combustion; they miss that fuel cells generate electricity directly from redox reactions, continuously refuelled.
The answer examiners want is: 96500. “8.31” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Cryolite (Na3AlF6). “Water only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Charge passed by 2 A for 100 s (C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
